question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a MITM attack on the unauthenticated mirror REST endpoint used by buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to inject fake node endpoints, causing submitWithRetry() to route all pinger transactions through a single attacker-chosen consensus node, thereby controlling the order in which transactions appear in the mirror node's transaction history without stealing funds?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the gap between Execute(client) succeeding and GetReceipt(client) failing (e.g., via network disruption) to cause submitWithRetry() to retry and submit a second distinct TransferTransaction for the same logical tick, resulting in two separate transaction IDs appearing in the mirror node history for what should have been a single transfer event, reorganizing the expected one-per-tick history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that GetReceipt(client) is not passed a context and therefore cannot be cancelled, causing the goroutine to block indefinitely after ctx is cancelled, while a concurrent ticker fires and submits a new transaction, creating out-of-order or overlapping transaction entries in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user perform DNS poisoning against the mirror REST URL (cfg.mirrorRest) to redirect buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to a malicious server that returns a crafted nodesEnvelope with all transactions routed to a single slow consensus node, causing the pinger's transactions to be sequenced differently than expected in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS certificate pinning in the http.Client used by fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to perform a TLS interception attack, injecting a crafted nodesEnvelope that maps all node account IDs to a single endpoint, forcing all pinger transactions through one node and altering the apparent ordering of transactions in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: backoff()] Can an unprivileged external user cause repeated transient failures in GetReceipt(client) (e.g., by disrupting network connectivity at precise intervals) to drive submitWithRetry() through all cfg.maxRetries attempts, each creating a new TransferTransaction with a distinct transaction ID, resulting in up to maxRetries+1 history entries per tick interval instead of one, reorganizing the expected linear transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that each retry in submitWithRetry() calls hiero.NewTransferTransaction() to create a brand-new transaction (not a re-submission of the same ID), so that a network disruption after Execute() but before GetReceipt() causes the mirror node to record multiple distinct successful transactions per tick, reorganizing the one-per-interval history invariant?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a crafted HTTP response from a MITM position that includes a nodesEnvelope with a Links.Next pagination pointer, knowing that fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() never follows the next link, causing the pinger to build an incomplete node network map that omits certain consensus nodes, thereby biasing all future transactions toward specific nodes and altering the distribution of transactions across nodes in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted nodesEnvelope response (via MITM or DNS poisoning) where multiple nodeEntry records share the same NodeAccountID but different ServiceEndpoints, causing the network map to be overwritten with the last-seen endpoint for that account ID, effectively hiding some consensus nodes from the pinger and forcing transactions to specific nodes that affect their ordering in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted nodesEnvelope where the DomainName field of a serviceEndpoint contains an attacker-controlled hostname, causing net.JoinHostPort() to build a connection string pointing to attacker infrastructure, so that the pinger's transactions are submitted to a fake node that delays or reorders them before forwarding to the real network, reorganizing their appearance in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted nodesEnvelope where the IPAddressV4 field contains an attacker-controlled IP address (since there is no IP validation beyond checking for empty string), causing the pinger to route transactions through attacker-controlled infrastructure that can delay or reorder them, altering the sequence of transactions recorded in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&payload) has no body size limit to send an extremely large nodesEnvelope response (via MITM), causing the pinger process to consume excessive memory and potentially crash or stall, interrupting the regular transaction submission cadence and creating gaps in the mirror node transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the HTTP redirect-following behavior of the default http.Client in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to redirect the pinger's mirror REST request to an attacker-controlled server that returns a crafted node topology, causing all subsequent pinger transactions to be routed through attacker-chosen nodes and reorganizing their order in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the URL construction logic (strings.HasSuffix(base, '/api/v1')) in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() by influencing cfg.mirrorRest to end with '/api/v1' pointing to an attacker-controlled server, bypassing the '/api/v1' suffix appending and causing the pinger to fetch node topology from an attacker-controlled endpoint that returns a crafted network map reorganizing transaction routing?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ticker in main.go fires independently of whether the previous submitWithRetry() call has completed, causing concurrent in-flight TransferTransactions when a transfer takes longer than cfg.interval, resulting in transactions being submitted and confirmed out of the expected sequential order in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause selective packet loss on the gRPC connection to the consensus node after Execute(client) is called but before the response is received, causing Execute() to return an error even though the transaction was submitted, leading submitWithRetry() to retry with a new transaction, creating duplicate history entries for the same logical transfer event?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: backoff()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit integer overflow in backoff() where base * time.Duration(1<<(attempt-1)) overflows for large attempt values (e.g., attempt >= 64), causing the backoff duration to become negative or zero, making submitWithRetry() retry immediately without delay and flooding the network with rapid-fire transactions that appear as a burst in the mirror node history, reorganizing the expected evenly-spaced transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that AddHbarTransfer() uses client.GetOperatorAccountID() (resolved at transaction build time) and toID (resolved once at function entry) to craft a scenario where the operator account ID changes between retries (e.g., via concurrent config reload), causing transactions with different source accounts to appear in the mirror node history under the same logical tick?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a unique memo or nonce in hiero.NewTransferTransaction() to make all pinger transactions indistinguishable in the mirror node history, preventing auditors from determining which transactions are retries versus new submissions, effectively obscuring the true transaction history reorganization caused by retry storms?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the retry logic in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() (which retries on HTTP 429 or 5xx) by controlling a mirror REST endpoint to return 429 responses until the pinger's startup is delayed, causing the pinger to miss its first several tick intervals and creating gaps in the expected continuous transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger logs transaction IDs only on success (log.Printf with receipt.Status and resp.TransactionID) but not on failed Execute() calls, causing failed-then-retried transactions to be invisible in logs while still appearing in the mirror node history, making it impossible to correlate log entries with the actual transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cfg.amountTinybar is not validated to be positive (only non-zero is checked in loadConfig()), allowing a negative amountTinybar to reverse the transfer direction (operator receives, toAccount sends), creating history entries that appear as incoming transfers to the operator account, reorganizing the expected outgoing-only transfer history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hardcoded default operatorKey in config.go (302e020100300506032b65700422042091132178e72057a1d7528025956fe39b0b847f200ab59b2fdd367017f3087137) to submit their own transactions from the same operator account ID (0.0.2), interleaving attacker-controlled transactions with pinger transactions in the mirror node history and reorganizing the expected pinger-only transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the default toAccountID (0.0.98) and operatorID (0.0.2) in config.go to predict the exact transfer pattern of the pinger and submit their own transactions between pinger ticks, inserting attacker-controlled history entries between legitimate pinger entries and reorganizing the expected transaction sequence in the mirror node history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() skips nodeEntry records where NodeAccountID is empty (continue) but does not skip records where NodeAccountID fails AccountIDFromString() parsing (also continue), allowing a malicious mirror REST response to include entries with syntactically valid but semantically wrong account IDs that map to attacker-controlled endpoints, routing pinger transactions to attacker infrastructure?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a MITM attack on the unauthenticated mirror REST endpoint used by buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to inject fake node endpoints, causing submitWithRetry() to route all pinger transactions through a single attacker-chosen consensus node, thereby controlling the order in which transactions appear in the mirror node's transaction history without stealing funds?
